attributes.stem: An otherwise well 55-year old, post-menopausal woman presents with a painless lump in her left breast. On examination, there is a 3cm diameter firm, irregular mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast, tethered to the underlying muscle and 4cm from the nipple. Examination of the left axilla reveals palpable nodes in the apical and pectoral groups. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. Diagnostic mammography shows an irregular, spiculated mass with microcalcifications present. Breast ultrasound confirms three separate foci suspicious for carcinoma in the left upper outer quadrant. Core biopsy of the main mass confirms invasive ductal carcinoma which is oestrogen receptor (ER)/progesterone receptor (PR) negative and HER2 receptor positive. Fine needle biopsy of one of the enlarged axillary lymph nodes confirms malignant cells.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial neoadjuvant therapy?
attributes.explanation:
The standard of care for the case described here would be:

  • Neoadjuvant chemotherapy

  • Mastectomy and axillary staging

  • Adjuvant radiotherapy

  • Delayed breast reconstruction

Some women feel that they could not cope with a flat chest wall and for these women, a 'reverse sequence' is made to their treatment:

  • Neoadjuvant chemotherapy

  • Neoadjuvant radiotherapy

  • Mastectomy and axillary staging with immediate breast reconstruction.

So, while radiotherapy can be given in the neoadjuvant setting for a specific cohort of women, it is not the correct neoadjuvant treatment for this patient.

Breast conservation surgery (wide local excision) and mastectomy remain the two surgical options for early breast cancer. When a tumour is small and is localised, breast conservation is preferred. Whereas mastectomy is recommended if a tumour is larger or has multiple foci that involve two or more quadrants of the breast.

Assessing whether cancer has spread to the axillary lymph nodes is a key part of breast cancer staging. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is suitable for the majority of women who do not have metastatic spread to lymph nodes. However if there is evidence of invasion of lymph nodes then complete axillary lymph node clearance is indicated.

Medical adjuvant therapy for breast cancer is based upon the hormone receptor status. For triple negative, or ER/PR/HER2 negative cancers, as well as HER2 positive tumours, neoadjuvant chemotherapy is recommended prior to surgical management. When a patient is given neoadjuvant chemotherapy and no residual cancer is found during subsequent surgery, the patient is said to have a pathological complete response, and as a result have a significantly higher survival rate. If this is not achieved, then there is the possibility to give additional chemotherapy after surgical management. ER and PR positive tumours are responsive to anti-oestrogen therapy which includes either aromatase inhibitors, such as anastrozole, or selective oestrogen receptor modulators, such as tamoxifen. Aromatase inhibitors are reserved for post-menopausal women given that it prevents conversion of androgens to oestrogen peripherally. Although both selective oestrogen receptor modulators and aromatase inhibitors are suitable endocrine therapies for ER positive post-menopausal women, aromatase inhibitors are preferred as they are associated with a reduced recurrence risk when compared to selective oestrogen receptor modulators in this patient cohort. For this particular patient, the most appropriate neoadjuvant treatment would be chemotherapy and trastuzumab.

Trastuzumab is a monoclonal antibody used for patients with HER2 positive breast cancer which has been shown to improve the disease‑free survival and overall survival when used in combination with neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The significant adverse effect of trastuzumab – especially when used in combination with anthracycline chemotherapy – is the risk of cardiac toxicity and reduction in ejection fraction.

This patient has evidence of multifocal invasive carcinoma and positive pathological axillary lymph nodes. Thus the most appropriate surgical option is a mastectomy and axillary node clearance. Wide local excision of the carcinoma is not appropriate, given this carcinoma is invasive with multiple foci and axillary involvement. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is also not appropriate, as the presence of metastases in the axillary lymph nodes has already been confirmed. If there had been no evidence of lymph node involvement, then a mastectomy and sentinel node biopsy would have been the best option.
learningPoints: The choice of neoadjuvant therapy for breast cancer will be based on the hormone receptor status.
references: Heilat G, Brennan M, French J. Management of early stage breast cancer. Australian Journal for General Practitioners. 2019;48:604-8.
